news_communities.title: Swampscott man meets good Samaritan who saved his life
news_communities.author: Malcolm Johnson
news_communities.summary:
A Swampscott, Massachusetts, man who nearly lost his life met the people who saved it on Wednesday.

In mid-August, Tom Creamer and his wife Mary were wrapping up a busy day of errands.

“She wanted to go home but I had a letter to mail,” Creamer explained.

He walked to the Swampscott Post Office to mail his letter, and that’s all he remembers.

 “I don’t remember much of that day but it was a bit of closure to know where I fell to see that I almost made it into the post office to get that letter mailed,” he said.

Creamer collapsed. He was unconscious and unresponsive. That’s when Kate Sheer noticed him on the ground

“It was kind of a shock. I didn’t really know what to do,” she said.

Sheer began performing CPR before paramedics arrived.

“I was really scared but I felt like things were going to be OK,” she said.

Officer Michael Kenyon arrived first.

“When I arrived on scene, he wasn’t breathing and had a very, very faint pulse.”

Moments later, the fire department arrived and continued lifesaving measures. It’s a moment two strangers, bonded through an emergency, will never forget.

“I’m so thankful that everything turned out as it did. Honestly,” Sheer said.

“I appreciate everything that you’ve done because if it wasn’t for the Good Samaritan in you and starting the process with the chest compressions, I probably wouldn’t have made it,” Creamer told Sheer.

news_boston.title: Mass. State Police sergeant indicted in deadly 2023 Woburn crash
news_boston.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_boston.summary:
A Massachusetts State Police sergeant has been indicted in a deadly 2023 crash, the Suffolk County District Attorney said Thursday.

Sgt. Scott Quigley is charged with felony motor vehicle homicide in the Dec. 12, 2023, crash in Woburn.

According to investigators, Quigley hit a van that was carrying a 37-year-old man with disabilities. That man, Angelo Schettino, died a month later, on Jan. 13, 2024, of injuries related to the crash. The driver of the van was also hurt.

Quigley, who was off-duty at the time of the crash, was later named as a defendant in a wrongful death lawsuit brought by Schettino’s estate.

The matter came to public attention during an unrelated murder case in Lowell District Court, where Quigley was an investigator. Defense attorneys pointed to the accusations made against Quigley as a question of credibility, alleging that State Police may have covered up information about one of their own.

Middlesex District Attorney Marian Ryan said that her office was unaware of the allegations against Quigley until jury impalement in the murder case. The office referred the matter to Suffolk County in January.

An investigation into other aspects of the case, including its initial handling by State Police, is ongoing. Among the unanswered questions is whether Quigley was driving drunk. According to the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office, his blood alcohol content was reported at .11 – over the legal limit of .08.

Quigley received a warning for crossing marked lanes after the crash and was on injury leave for eight months.

Massachusetts State Police have said that they would initiate an independent investigation.

news_boston.title: Acton man missing since October found in Mass. lake
news_boston.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker
news_boston.summary:
The body of a man who had been missing since last October was found in a Massachusetts lake Wednesday.

The Worcester County District Attorney’s Office said Leonard Mercury, 57, of Acton, was last seen leaving Heywood Hospital in Gardner shortly before 2 a.m. on Oct. 31, 2025. The Gardner Police Department initially conducted search efforts for him but was unsuccessful in locating him.

Several other searches were conducted in the following months by multiple other agencies including the Westminster Police Department, the Massachusetts Environmental Police, and the Massachusetts State Police dive team, special emergency response team, marine and K-9 units.

Gardner police conducted an aerial search of Crystal Lake on March 25 using drone technology in connection to Mercury’s, and the drones revealed what appeared to be a human body floating in the water, according to officials.

State police divers recovered the body, which has since been identified as Mercury.

The chief medical examiner’s office will determine the cause and manner of Mercury’s death, officials added.

It’s unclear at this time why he left Heywood Hospital, how he ended up in the lake and how long he’s been dead.

This incident remains under investigation by state and local police, as well as the district attorney’s office.

news_boston.title: TSA boss warns of airport closures as Homeland Security funding fight drags on
news_boston.author: Lisa Mascaro and Kevin Freking | The Associated Press
news_boston.summary:
The Transportation Security Administration may have to shut down operations at some airports if the budget impasse drags on, the agency’s acting head said Wednesday, even as record wait time for travelers did little to end the standoff over the funding fight in Congress.

The TSA’s Ha Nguyen McNeill described the mounting hardships facing unpaid airport workers — piling up bills and eviction notices, even plasma donations to make ends meet — and warned that lawmakers must ensure “this never happens again.”

McNeil noted that they are already being forced to consolidate lanes and warned of potential closures of smaller airports if they don’t have enough officers.

“This is a dire situation,” she testified at a House hearing. “At this point, we have to look at all options on the table. And that does require us to, at some point, make very difficult choices as to which airports we might try to keep open and which ones we might have to shut down as our callout rates increase.”

Yet on the 41st day of the standoff involving the Department of Homeland Security, there was no easy way out in sight. Neither Republican senators, who made the latest offer, nor Democrats, who countered by reiterating their demands for changes to President Donald Trump ‘s immigration enforcement operations, appeared closer to a compromise.

Trump, who initially appeared to have given his nod to the deal, has declined to lend it his full support or put his political weight behind making sure it is approved.

Top officials at agencies under the DHS umbrella spoke for more than three-hours before the House Homeland Security Committee about the potential risks of security lapses unless the partial government shutdown comes to an end.

Air travel Mar 25

‘Feed our kids or come to work': TSA officers share how they're scraping by without pay

Air travel Mar 24

How early you should arrive to the airport amid TSA delays, hourslong lines

A deal teeters on collapse

DHS has gone without routine funding since mid-February. Democrats are insisting on changes to the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement and mass deportation operations after the killings of two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis by federal officers during protests.

The latest GOP proposal would fund most of DHS except for the enforcement and removal operations of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement that have been central to the debate. The plan would provide money for other aspects of ICE as well as Customs and Border Protection.

While the offer added some new restraints on immigration officers, including the use of body cameras, it excluded other policies that Democrats have demanded, such as requirements that federal agents wear identification and refrain from conducting raids around schools, churches or other sensitive places.

Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer of New York said they needed to see real changes. “We’ve been talking about ICE reforms from day one,” he said.

Republican leaders said Democrats are putting the country at risk.

“They know this is crazy,” said House Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La.

But conservative Republicans also panned the proposal, demanding full funding for immigration operations and skeptical of the promise from GOP leaders that they would address Trump’s proof-of-citizenship voting bill in a subsequent legislative package.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune said late Wednesday that if Democrats put a “more realistic offer on the table, we’ll be back in business.”

Airport lines grow as TSA workers endure hardships

McNeill, the acting TSA administrator, told lawmakers that multiple airports are experiencing greater than 40% callout rates and more than 480 transportation security officers have now quit during the shutdown.

She cited the growing financial strain on the TSA workforce.

“Some are sleeping in their cars, selling their blood and plasma, and taking on second jobs to make ends meet, all while being expected to perform at the highest level when in uniform to protect the traveling public,” she said.

McNeil also said TSA officers working at the nation’s airports have experienced a more than 500% increase in the frequency of assaults since the shutdown began.

“This is unacceptable and it will not be tolerated,” McNeill said.

The top executive overseeing Houston’s airport said security lines that have travelers waiting four hours or more could get longer if the political impasse was not soon settled.

Lines that twist and turn across multiple floors at George Bush Intercontinental Airport have been the result of TSA only being able to staff one-third to one-half the usual number of checkpoint lines, said Jim Szczesniak, aviation director for Houston’s airport system.

Trump’s decision to send ICE agents to the airports risks inflaming the situation, lawmakers have said. Video footage of federal officers detaining a crying woman at San Francisco International Airport drew outrage Monday from local officials, although it was unrelated to Trump’s order to deploy immigration officers.

FEMA also at risk

The Federal Emergency Management Agency’s Disaster Relief Fund is “rapidly depleting,” Victoria Barton, a FEMA external affairs official, told lawmakers.

FEMA is able to continue its disaster response and recovery work as long as that fund has money, and about 10,000 of its disaster workers continue being paid through it.

___

Associated Press writers Wyatte Grantham-Philips in New York, Rio Yamat in Las Vegas, Russ Bynum in Houston and Gabriela Aoun Angueira in San Diego contributed to this report.

news_boston.title: 9‑year‑old powerlifter stuns internet with 180‑pound deadlift in viral video
news_boston.author: Julia Elbaba
news_boston.summary:
Don’t let the pigtails fool you, 9-year-old Lucy Milgrim is a powerhouse.

The New York native stunned the internet after a video of her deadlifting 180 pounds at the Arnold Sports Festival in Columbus, Ohio, racked up nearly 60 million views.

Milgrim, who weighs under 60 pounds, stepped up to the bar in fluorescent pink‑and‑blue shoes, a powerlifting belt, and her signature pigtails. With “Thunderstruck” blasting in the background, she locked in, bent her knees and grabbed the bar.

In one powerful motion, she pulled the weight to her waist, completing the remarkable lift. The 9‑year‑old broke into a huge smile and threw her arms up to flex her muscles.

When it comes to competition, the Milgrim told USA Today that she feels a great sense of accomplishment when she hits a “really big weight.”

In addition to her deadlift, the powerlifter has squatted 150 pounds and bench pressed 85 pounds, she told the outlet. During her first year of powerlifting, she set three American Records through the USA Powerlifting organization, her mother, Michelle, added.

To put her strength in perspective, many children her age are still learning basic form with light weights, not hoisting more than triple their bodyweight.

“People see these numbers, and they say, ‘Wow, she must be training really hard to get that number.’ The truth is, she just has a different baseline than most kids,” her father, Brett, told USA Today. “Lucy’s always been this really naturally strong kid.”

Besides powerlifting, Milgrim is passionate about wrestling, something her parents inspired her to pursue.

“My mom and dad; they were working out in the gym, and I saw them doing all this cool stuff, and I wanted to try it too,” she explained. “I got interested in wrestling because my dad is a wrestling coach and he used to bring me and my brother to wrestling practices.”

Milgrim has been wrestling competitively for three years and currently practices four or five days a week. Besides her father, she is coached by Vougar Oroudjov, who won the bronze medal for light-flyweight wrestling at the 1992 Barcelona Summer Olympics.

At the end of the day, her love for the sport comes from a youthful place. 

“My favorite part of wrestling is when you get to hang out with all your friends, and when you win the match, you get your hand raised,” Milgrim said.

And despite being an internet sensation, she is still very much a kid at the end of the day.

“She doesn’t look at comments. She doesn’t know amounts of followers or views or any of that. She’s a 9-year-old girl. She does 9-year-old girl things,” Brett said. “She’ll go to wrestling practices, and she’ll come home and put on a dress and … play with her little sister.”

news_boston.title: Former Boston school bus driver arraigned in crash that killed kindergartener
news_boston.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Staff Reports
news_boston.summary:
A former Boston school bus driver indicted in a tragic crash that killed a kindergartener in Hyde Park last April was arraigned on Thursday.

Jean Charles, 39, of Brockton, faces charges of involuntary manslaughter, reckless motor vehicular homicide and negligent motor vehicle homicide in the crash that killed 5-year-old Lens A. Joseph on April 28, 2025, according to the Suffolk County District Attorney’s Office.

Investigators have previously said that a school bus struck Lens on Washington Street in Hyde Park at about 2:45 p.m. that Monday as children were being dropped off from school. He was with his 11-year-old cousin, who could be heard screaming for help after the crash. Lens’ uncle said the young boy was trying to cross the street in front of the bus to get home when he was struck.

Lens, who was a student at the Up Academy in Dorchester, was rushed to Boston Children’s Hospital, where he was pronounced dead.

Prosecutors, during the arraignment on Thursday, laid out a series of alleged missteps by Charles that broke policy and procedure. Among them was allegedly skipping the pre-trip inspection, which prosecutors said would have noted a broken safety crossing bar and a flat rear-left tire, missing bus stops, and failing to keep track of the location of children as he dropped them off.

“At no time did the defendant look to see where Lens was or to account for his whereabouts or check his mirrors,” Assistant District Attorney Ursula Knight said. “Additionally, had the safety crossing bar been working properly, something the defendant was obligated to assure, it would have prevented Lens from walking in close to the front of the bus. Without that safeguard, Lens cut directly across and in front of the bus.”

Charles’ attorney Kenneth Anderson said the defense views the case as a “tragic accident.”

“Our hearts go out to the family of Lens Arthur Joseph, we can’t imagine the loss they’re going through,” Anderson said.

Charles was held on an agreed-upon $15,000 bail following his arraignment in Suffolk Superior Court on Thursday.

Matthew Fogelman, the Joseph family attorney, released the following statement after the arraignment:

“While nothing will bring back our beloved Lens, we take some measure of comfort in the criminal justice system’s efforts to hold the driver responsible for his own actions. And now the criminal process will proceed. Meanwhile, we will continue to pursue accountability for Lens’ death against Transdev, the bus company, via the civil justice system.” 

The fatal crash was caught on the bus’ dashboard camera, which was previously shared by lawyers representing the boy’s family in a civil lawsuit against Transdev.

Charles resigned on May 14, just before a termination hearing was set to start.

At the time, he was an employee of Transdev and had been driving BPS buses for two years. He had an expired state-required certification on the day of the crash. School bus certificates are needed as part of the requirements to drive a school bus in Massachusetts, and Transdev had notified Charles that he needed to renew his certification, but he did not.

The crash led Boston Public Schools to commission an investigation into the incident and into school bus safety. The final report included recommendations for wide-scale reforms.

Attorneys for Lens’ family previously said in a statement that the tragedy “could have been, and should have been, completely avoided.”

“We are dedicated to pursuing justice for the families involved,” attorneys Matthew Fogelman, Alan Klevan and Jeffrey Simons said. “We are also dedicated to making changes to any policies and procedures to ensure that something as senseless as this never happens again.”

According to officials, Charles had been employed as a bus driver since May of 2023 and was assigned a regular daily route. On April 28, the day of the crash, Charles was assigned an additional route as a substitute driver to cover an early release from the Up Academy.

During the route, Charles hit another vehicle and failed to notify a Transdev safety supervisor, who would have decided whether he would complete the route or be replaced, in violation of protocol.

Charles continued on the route after choosing not to notify Transdev, and then he later missed the turn onto Glenwood Avenue, where Lens lived, and instead arrived at the intersection with Glenwood Avenue by turning onto Washington Street, where the tragedy occurred.

Transdev has previously disclosed that Charles’ driving record over the last two years includes four minor incidents prior to April 28, including two earlier in April 2025, after which Transdev pulled Charles off the road for two days for retraining. These include contact with a parked vehicle (May 22, 2024), contact with another car’s rear tire (December 3, 2024), contact with another car’s bumper (April 9, 2025), and contact with another vehicle’s side mirror (April 14, 2025).

Officials have also previously said that Charles did undergo drug and alcohol testing, with no sign of either found immediately after the fatal crash.

news_boston.title: Weezer announces new tour, includes September stop at TD Garden
news_boston.author: Marc Fortier
news_boston.summary:
Weezer announced a new North American tour Thursday, which includes a stop this September at Boston’s TD Garden.

“Weezer: The Gathering” is a 32-city arena tour, with special guests The Shins and Silversun Pickups. It is slated to make a stop at TD Garden on Sept. 27.

The band has sold over 35 million records worldwide, with hits like “Island In The Sun,” “Go Away,” “Buddy Holly” and more. The tour announcements comes as Weezer is set to release a new song, “Shine Again,” on April 1 from their upcoming, still-untitled album, which has yet to set a release date.

Tickets for all shows will be available starting with a Citi, Verizon, Mastercard, American Express, and Weezer fan club presale beginning on Tuesday, March 31, at 10 a.m. Additional presales will run throughout the week ahead of the general on-sale beginning on Friday, April 3, at 10 a.m. at Weezer.com.

news_sports.title: Series Preview: Boston Red Sox at Cincinnati Reds
news_sports.summary:
We’re back! For one afternoon. There there’s an off day. And then another game on Saturday. And at that point the baseball season will have successfully started like so many lawnmowers that sputter and make you pull the string a couple of times before they get going. (You probably don’t have to mow your lawn yet in the Boston area but watch out for many, many ads from Scott’s featuring the guy below.)

But no matter how many days it takes, this is Opening Weekend.

And the Red Sox are starting back where every Opening Day used to be played: Cincinnati.

The Reds are no longer the team of Joey Votto, who’s now a broadcaster on NBC. The Red Sox are looking to have their own Votto in Roman Anthony, a home grown (potential) star. And Anthony isn’t the only exciting name on the roster. Boston starts the season with Garrett Crochet taking the ball. Then trade acquisition Sonny Gray returns to the ballpark he called home from 2019-2021. And for the series finale: Connelly Early, who, because of injuries to the rotation last season, arrived a little ahead of schedule, and who will now make his first start following an Opening Day roster battle victory.

Boston faces a lefty, a righty, and another righty, for those of you counting at home trying to figure out when and where Marcelo Mayer, Jarren Duran, Wilyer Abreu, and Masataka Yoshida will play. I think we’ll see two games for Mayer (the lineup isn’t out as of writing). Will Cora quickly start that DH/outfield rotation to keep Masa hot after the WBC? We’re about to find out. He might become a pinch-hitting wonder. There’s so much to explore at the start of the season.

Who is going to run wild on the base paths?

Who has a breakout homer season?

Is there another campaign for a Cy Young — not from Crochet (though he will likely be a contender) — but from someone else 10 years after Rick Porcello surprised everyone? It could happen.

According to Ian Browne of MLB.com, the first pitch will be thrown out by none other than Bronson Arroyo. He wasn’t as much of a legend in Cincinnati but he definitely left his mark.

Probable Pitching Matchups

Thursday, March 26: Garrett Crochet (— ERA / — FIP) vs. Andrew Abbott (— ERA / — FIP)

Saturday, March 27: Sonny Gray (— ERA / — FIP) vs. Brady Singer (— ERA / — FIP)

Sunday, March 29: Connelly Early (— ERA / — FIP) vs. Rhett Lowder (— ERA / — FIP)

When/Where to Watch

Thursday, March 26 at 4:10 PM ET on NESN

Saturday, March 28: 4:10 PM ET on NESN

Sunday, March 29: 1:40 PM ET on NESN

news_bc.title: “Impossible Choices”: Newton Schools Face Budget Cuts Despite Funding Boost
news_bc.author: Leon Gopaul
news_bc.summary:
The Newton School Committee (NSC) met Monday to update the community on the district’s budget mandates, staff cuts that will increase responsibilities for remaining employees, and ongoing structural deficits.

“The current situation of our budget landscape is that we have been given a historic increase in our base allocation, and that gives us $17.1 million to work with,” said Newton Public Schools Superintendent Dr. Anna Nolin. “$12 million of which will go to mandated salary increases, $4.4 million to health care.”

Nolin emphasized that, although the budget allocation increased, the mandates leave the district with only about $700,000 for all other operations.

“That means that we have to make—at a minimum—$2.8 million of reductions,” Nolin said.

The reductions come after Newton Mayor Marc Laredo’s refusal to allocate an additional $2.8 million for the fiscal year 2027 (FY27) Newton Public Schools’ budget. 

Nolin also factored in an additional $1 million for academic program improvements currently under consideration, which would increase the reduction needed. She expressed concern over the sustainability of these programs.

“I’m worried we’re going to be sitting here next year talking about whether we can keep the math program going or the elementary program,” Nolin said.

Because there was a collective desire to protect the direct student experience, budget reductions have targeted central office roles instead, Nolin explained.

“All of these different directors and leaders and the folks who also serve under them, including secretaries or custodians, all made the list,” Nolin said. “In terms of the number, that is quite large, $2.7 million worth of reductions. That’s a lot of individuals.”

Nolin acknowledged that these reductions will change the distribution of work among employees.

“Things are going to go to different people,” Nolin said. “There’s going to be impacts on assistant superintendents, principals, the business office, and myself. There are lots of folks that are going to take on coordination roles for the district.”

Nolin raised the possibility of Newton becoming part of the state’s Inter-District School Choice program, which would allow students in other districts to attend Newton Public Schools. 

“I have to raise the specter of becoming a school choice district again so that we can bring in revenue on a regular basis and try to stabilize ourselves and get out of this situation we’re in,” Nolin said.

Converting to a school choice district would not only help alleviate the budget reductions they hope to make but also help fund mandates they have not been able to meet, Nolin explained. 

“I feel that we are at a place where we have to ask for new revenue sources because we are out of options with our old revenue sources,” said Nolin.

Nolin and the committee face a series of “impossible choices,” and she explained that protecting the student experience is the number one priority, and hard decisions are going to be made in the process.

“Every single decision here that we are faced with is just an impossible choice of what can be cut when any number of groups say that what is up for consideration is core to them, and core to our mission,” Nolin said.

The post “Impossible Choices”: Newton Schools Face Budget Cuts Despite Funding Boost appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: April Showers Bring May Flowers: Raincoats To Survive the Drizzly Season
news_bc.author: Katherine Phelps
news_bc.summary:
As a Connecticut native, I know a thing or two about New England weather. 

Many assume that winter at Boston College means lots of snow. While accurate, people forget about one thing: rain. From March to May, BC will be filled with puddles and umbrellas.

Wondering how to survive that weather? Here are the five best raincoats to battle the drizzly days headed your way.

The North Face Parka  

This North Face hooded parka—that happens to be waterproof—is perfect for the weird, chilly weather that gets mixed with rain in April. It’s as if we’re so close to warm weather, yet so far. Those warm summer days feel so far away when the rain starts to pour. 

The worst feeling is coming to class all sweaty in your raincoat when it’s humid out. So take my advice and use this jacket only for the days that land below 50 degrees. 

Take it from someone who owns this piece—it really is perfect for the transition from freezing snow to warmer skies ahead. 

Revolve Trench Raincoat 

Raincoats can sometimes feel like they’re ruining an outfit. I have personally suffered getting drenched a time or two just because I felt like an umbrella or coat simply did not match the vibe. 

But even on sunny days, I can almost guarantee that you won’t want to ditch this Revolve trench raincoat. The jacket is beyond adorable, and the jade gingham lining is the perfect hint of spring as we start seeing green grass again. 

This trench style dominated in the fall, and it’s safe to say it’ll stay a staple for the spring. 

Dairy Boy Jacket 

Dairy Boy is all the rage right now, and for good reason.

In comparison to some of the other trendy influencer brands, Dairy Boy is reasonably priced and of great quality. This barn jacket is the perfect staple for drizzly days, and it’s reversible. 

The corduroy collar and plaid detailing are classic and timeless. You can pair this jacket with your favorite rain boots, and you are good to go as you brave Boston’s unpredictable weather.

Patagonia Raincoat 

The Patagonia raincoat is especially fitting for those who stay active no matter the weather—the type of people who go for a run, walk, or bike ride in the rain because they love it. This may not be me, but I support it.

This jacket offers unzippable vents so you don’t overheat, or you can ditch the jacket altogether and make it portable by folding it into its pocket. 

But if there’s one thing I’ve learned since being in Boston, it is that the weather is weird. I mean, remember when it was 75 degrees one day and then 35 the next? Luckily, if you get caught in the cold, you can use the handwarmer zipped pockets to stay warm.

A Pop Of Color 

March and April can be very grey months here in Boston, but that does not mean your rain jacket needs to match that gloomy energy. 

This adorable Nordstrom coat is lightweight with toggles to adjust the waist so you don’t get lost in your jacket. It can also be washed in the instance that a rain puddle becomes your enemy and stains your jacket. 

A splash of color is always needed, and you are sure to brighten someone’s rainy day—or your own—with this London Fog raincoat.  

The post April Showers Bring May Flowers: Raincoats To Survive the Drizzly Season appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: BC Holds Off Northeastern 3–2 in Midweek Matchup
news_bc.author: Laney Halsey
news_bc.summary:
Boston College baseball flew into its game on Tuesday on a hot streak, having won five straight games, including a sweep of California last weekend.

The Eagles (17–8, 6–3 Atlantic Coast) did just enough to extend it, edging past rival Northeastern (12–10, 7–2 Coastal Athletic) 3–2 to win their sixth game in a row.

BC’s Jack Toomey opened the scoring at the top of the second, leading off the inning with a double down the left field line. Two batters later, Luke Gallo brought him home on a groundout to give BC a 1–0 lead.

“Luke getting the chopper in just kind of sets the tone, and anytime you get hard contact on the field early in the game, I think it just kind of gives you a little more control of what’s going on,” said BC head coach Todd Interdonato.

Northeastern answered in the third with a major threat, loading the bases with no outs. Harrison Feinberg laced a single to tie the game at 1–1, causing a pitching change as Luke D’Ancona was brought on in relief.

D’Ancona came through, inciting a baserunning error from the Huskies and getting a strikeout to end the inning. D’Ancona would earn the win, shoving 3.2 shutout innings and striking out six.

BC regained the lead in the fifth. Colin Larson walked and Julio Solier singled to give the Eagles runners at first and second with no outs. After a popout from Ty Mainolfi, Nick Wang singled to score Larson and put BC back on top.

“Some ballparks are just really hard to score in, and in my short time here, that’s a ballpark that’s just kind of hard to score in,” Interdonato said. “I think we punched out 15 or 16 batters, and, you know, I think that’s a credit to our pitchers executing what they were able to execute.”

Northeastern pitcher Cooper Maher limited the damage, catching Julio Solier stealing at third and keeping the Huskies within striking distance.

From there, BC’s pitching staff controlled the game. Northeastern’s offense, which scored 17 runs in its last game against Elon, didn’t score another run until the ninth. The Eagles struck out a season-high 16 Huskies while allowing just four hits.

“I think pitching has been exceptional,” Interdonato said. “I feel like whether we get a lead, like we did against Sacred Heart, and Lowell, we were able to keep in control, and then even when we’re down, like we were against Cal, those guys [have the] ability to keep the next run off the board, and just kind of keep it within striking distance.”

BC would get an important insurance run in the top of the ninth. Solier laid down a sacrifice bunt to score Owen DeShazo to put BC up 3–1.

The extra cushion would prove to be decisive. The Huskies sneaked in a final run courtesy a Chris Walsh RBI single, but one final strike out by Ryan Gerety would end the game in a 3–2 win for BC.

The post BC Holds Off Northeastern 3–2 in Midweek Matchup appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: UGBC Expresses Optimism for Flat-Rate Textbook Program, Discusses Nursing Transportation Initiative
news_bc.author: Danica Bergen
news_bc.summary:
UGBC senators said Tuesday night that administrators appeared supportive of a flat-rate textbook program at Boston College—although the Senate expressed doubts about whether the proposal could be implemented uniformly for all of BC’s schools.

“BC has actually explored this initiative before with UGBC, but it got no traction, just with the complexity of it and economic, kind of, uncertainty,” said Thomas Gray, student senator and MCAS ’27.

The initiative, which Gray introduced in February, would let students pay a flat-rate fee each semester for all required readings through a “Pay One” program. 

A similar system is already present at several universities, including the University of Arizona, from which Gray transferred.

Gray said that during a meeting with Vice Provost for Faculties Billy Soo, he noted that Morrissey College of Arts and Sciences classes “naturally have less or less expensive textbooks” and UGBC would likely be “subsidizing [the Carroll School of Management] with a flat-rate textbook program.”

To circumvent the issue, Gray said an alternative proposal could limit the initiative to just CSOM or have the University implement a separate fee for each school.

“So exploring possibilities about maybe only CSOM has the flat-rate, or just having separate flat-rate programs are possible,” said Gray.

The Connell School of Nursing already utilizes a flat-rate textbook program for undergraduate students, according to Gray, though he said the current system is not streamlined. 

“They literally send a spreadsheet of who’s opting out from the textbook flat rate, which is sent to the bookstore, which is sent to the publisher,” said Gray. “It’s very inefficient and outdated.”

To implement a similar University-wide system, Gray said the structure would need to improve on CSON’s current model.

“So if we want to scale this, we have to get a fully automated system,” said Gray.

According to Gray, students would automatically be enrolled in a Pay One program and could opt out. 

Gray noted that this system would be significantly more convenient for BC students.

“Either your textbooks are already uploaded to Canvas, and you don’t have to go searching through your syllabus, syllabi, and things like that, and everything’s set to go,” said Gray.

Jordan Doty, student senator and LSHED ’28, said the program would be convenient—especially around the beginning of the semester.

“But to another point, at least in my classes, like the first two weeks, you cannot get books,” she said. “I think that [program] would streamline the process.”

Doty predicted that BC faculty would back the program.

“There’ll be professors being like ‘Okay, guys, like do you have this book?’ [And students say] like, ‘oh, I got this version.’ And now all our page numbers are different,” said Doty. “So I think faculty could, I could see them potentially backing this.”

Additionally, UGBC is searching for a way to provide all undergraduate nursing students with free, accessible transportation to clinicals. 

CSON students must complete 1,000 hours of clinical experience in off-campus hospital settings, with transportation costs not currently covered.

UGBC discussed modeling a potential CSON nursing transportation program after the PULSE program. 

Currently, PULSE students receive a $50 CharlieCard each semester for transportation to and from their service. 

“We kind of just found out that the funding comes from BC itself, and it’s been like that for as long as the PULSE program’s really been around,” said Emily Prucher, MCAS ’28. 

Prucher noted, however, that BC intentionally chooses PULSE sites near T stops to make transportation to service more convenient, though that is not always the case for clinical program locations, which could pose a roadblock for the initiative.

“We talked through that they always choose stops that are convenient for students to get to, right on the T lines,” said Prucher. “But I know the nursing stops aren’t like that, like even mostly hospitals you can’t really get to.”

Prucher said that UGBC will continue to explore options for a CSON transportation program to clinicals and “reach out to some new contacts.”

Correction (3/25/26, 9:50 a.m.): This article was corrected to note that Billy Soo is the vice provost for the University, rather than the Carroll School of Management. 

The post UGBC Expresses Optimism for Flat-Rate Textbook Program, Discusses Nursing Transportation Initiative appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Broadcasting The Troubles: BC Professor Honored by National University of Ireland
news_bc.author: Henry Eig
news_bc.summary:
Ireland isn’t always considered a heavy-hitter on the global stage. 

Even Boston College’s Robert Savage, a member of the history and Irish studies faculty and scholar of modern Irish, British, and Atlantic world history, acknowledges this reality.

“It’s tiny,” Savage said. “It’s a small country. It has no significant military.”

Yet Savage, who has made it his life’s work to study the complexities of 20th-century Ireland, believes that the Emerald Isle “punches above its weight” in terms of global influence and lasting legacy.

Last November, the National University of Ireland awarded Savage an honorary Doctor of Literature degree for his publications focusing on the country and its history.

Savage’s publications concentrate on the role of broadcast media during the Troubles, a period of sectarian conflict in Northern Ireland that spanned from the 1960s to the 1990s. 

His interest in the field began as a high school and college student.

“There wasn’t the kind of multiple, you know, fractured media landscape that we have today,” Savage said. “The news that was broadcast every night here in the U.S., or in Australia, or France, or England, would cover events all over the place.”

Savage, watching from the United States, took a special interest in the two-to-three-minute sections that provided updates on the violence in Northern Ireland. 

“I was always fascinated by how those reports were put together,” Savage said. “And what I found myself doing is going into the archive and reading how reporters, cameramen, editors, and managers all decided what could be broadcast and how it could be broadcasted.” 

Savage found many troubling accounts of tension as independent media correspondents, such as the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC), clashed with political and governmental groups while reporting on the conflict.

“The British government was always sensitive about how the BBC—whether it was television or radio—would report on the conflict in Northern Ireland,” Savage said. “The British government was always focused on trying to encourage a narrative that presented London as peacemakers, trying to keep warring sides apart.”

According to Savage, this narrative came into direct conflict with how many broadcasts covered the Troubles. The BBC, for example, often reported critically on British policy, tactics, and initiatives in Northern Ireland.

On multiple occasions, major backlash followed news reports that expressed any degree of sympathy with the Irish Republican Army (IRA), Savage said.

“[The British government] regarded anybody in the IRA as a criminal, terrorist, and a dangerous person, whereas the BBC was trying to understand what motivated the IRA, why they were using violence, and why they had support from their communities,” Savage said.

“

[The British government] regarded anybody in the IRA as a criminal, terrorist, and a dangerous person, whereas the BBC was trying to understand what motivated the IRA, why they were using violence, and why they had support from their communities.

— Robert Savage

Savage’s two most recent books examine the role of the BBC during the Troubles.

Dominic Bryan, a professor at Queen’s University Belfast, praised Savage’s 2022 publication, Northern Ireland, the BBC and Censorship in Thatcher’s Britain.

“One of the things I love about Rob’s work is that he represents the relationship between both Britain and Ireland by taking both states very seriously,” Bryan said. “In other words, if you look at his work on the BBC, he understands the impact that Ireland has had on an institution which is at the core of the United Kingdom and Britain, and is, in fact, also hugely influential in Ireland.”

Byran isn’t only an expert with an experienced view of Savage’s research. He also played a key role in helping Savage uncover the realities hidden behind the BBC’s polished broadcasts. 

“I happened to be a filing clerk in the BBC at the time—it was sort of my first summer job when I was a student, and I was looking at all of these files for the director general,” Bryan said. “So I always smile because that means that Rob is looking at the papers that I had originally filed.”

Bryan pointed Savage toward moments that had been especially tense between the company and the British state—moments that, at times, brought up complicated ethical dilemmas.

“So the BBC, Rob writes, attempts on a number of occasions to dig deep into the Republican movement to understand it, which a lot of us would think is a good thing—we need to understand it,” Bryan said. “But of course, that means you’re putting the views out of people who were killing British soldiers.”

Bryan looks back on his long-lasting relationship with Savage with appreciation.

“Not only has he been an important figure in developing aspects of his history on Ireland, but he’s been an important figure in Irish studies generally, and an important figure across the Atlantic, with the amount of work that he’s done,” Bryan said.

“

Not only has he been an important figure in developing aspects of his history on Ireland, but he’s been an important figure in Irish studies generally, and an important figure across the Atlantic, with the amount of work that he’s done.

— Dominic Bryan

Beyond his publications, Savage presents the unique role of media in Ireland to his classes at BC.

In one case, students studied how, in the 1960s, CBS was developing a documentary on Ireland. Based on archival material, students learned that the Irish government fought against the network, arguing for a more positive portrayal of the country.

“CBS was saying, ‘We want to look at Ireland as it is, not as how you’d like it to be,’” Savage said. “’So we’d like to talk about immigration and poverty and the challenges the government faces regarding Northern Ireland.’”

Every time Savage returns from an archive in Ireland or the UK, he brings back digital copies of documents for BC undergraduates to analyze.

“Instead of just reading a textbook, students are able to read these correspondences to get a sense of what was really going on,” Savage said. 

Savage attributes much of this hands-on teaching approach to BC’s academic environment, explaining that the University has funded his faculty fellowships in Galway, Edinburgh, Belfast, and Dublin over his last three decades as a professor.

“BC is a great place to work because the University understands scholarship and really invests in its faculty,” Savage said. “They’re all about getting away so you can do the research needed to write books, but also to teach better courses.”

In Savage’s opinion, his recent successes can be traced back to BC’s devotion to thoughtful scholarly output. His honorary doctorate was facilitated, at least in part, by their investment. 

“It’s wonderful to be recognized, but it’s imperative to also recognize that the award wouldn’t be possible without the help of BC,” Savage said. 

As a Boston native, Savage brings a foreign perspective to the field of Irish studies.

“As an outsider, you don’t grow up in that society, so you don’t experience things the way that Irish citizens might,” Savage said. “You come with a different kind of perspective, and I think that can be rewarding, but it can also be a challenge.”

Rowan Bianchi, a Ph.D. candidate and teaching assistant in BC’s history department, agrees that an American background can make for both a unique yet rewarding experience—especially in BC’s Irish studies program.

“One of the reasons I wanted to come to Boston College to study Irish history was because I knew that BC has this really interesting position where we have all these connections in Ireland,” Bianchi said. “We’re outsiders, but we’re outsiders that have a specific reputation.” 

Although Bianchi’s research focus predates Savage’s by almost a century, the two have still crossed paths in the Irish studies program. They spoke to his wealth of resources in terms of primary documents. 

“While he mostly does stuff on broadcast, he’s also really knowledgeable about newspapers, which are a major source that I use,” Bianchi said.

With a lifetime’s work in media studies, Savage has gained a reputation among his peers for a deep understanding of 20th-century news networks. 

“We always joke that he’s the founder of ‘BBC studies’ here because he’s such an expert on them,” Bianchi said. “I swear, he knows every employee that worked there during the 1970s—could probably list them off the top of his head.”

Savage’s award is not just a reflection of BC’s scholarship, but of his personal commitment to Irish studies, Bianchi said.

“He has been such a force for putting Boston College’s name out there in Ireland,” Bianchi said. “I feel like he’s such a great representative of the best of the best that they have to offer.”

“

He has been such a force for putting Boston College’s name out there in Ireland.

— Rowan Bianchi

Savage gives credit to the interdisciplinary nature of BC’s Irish studies program.

“Irish studies is not just historians, its faculty are in English, in fine art, in media studies, in philosophy, and in music,” Savage said. “Bringing all those disciplines together enables a more comprehensive understanding of Ireland.”

Ultimately, Savage stresses that there’s always a lesson to be learned from history—especially the history of news and media.

“We get a very sanitized version, oftentimes, of the conflict in the Middle East or in other parts of the world,” Savage said. “But what matters is to be skeptical and to appreciate the need for independent broadcasting that’s free of censorship.”

The post Broadcasting The Troubles: BC Professor Honored by National University of Ireland appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: BC Pro Day 2026: “It Would Mean Everything.”
news_bc.author: Mac Cobb
news_bc.summary:
There was a recurring theme from the players who spoke at Boston College football’s 2026 Pro Day—it was great to come back home and show what they could do in front of players and coaches they knew well.

“I think I did a lot better when it comes to movement than I did at the combine,” offensive tackle Jude Bowry said. “I was more comfortable in the atmosphere, old teammates, new teammates, coming in and showing support.”

It was an especially big day for wide receiver Lewis Bond, who was not invited to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine despite setting BC’s single-season (88) and all-time (213) reception records, plus earning All-ACC Second Team honors.

“I was proud to showcase what I could do,” Bond said. “Just to showcase what I could do, prove that I could run routes, prove that I could play at the next level.”

BC head coach Bill O’Brien had some high praise for Bond, too. 

“Lewis Bond is one of the best players to ever play here,” O’Brien said. “For him not to be invited to the combine … I think Lewis has a real good shot to get drafted.”

While Bond performed well on Monday in Fish Field House, he is glad to move past the metrics and measurements of Pro Day and focus on playing football again.

“This was probably the hardest part—this is the non-football part,” Bond said. “It’s good to be over. It’s good to be back to football.”

The players also talked about how important it is to have a legacy of former BC players in the NFL to lean on, especially last year’s selections: Donovan Ezeiruaku, Ozzy Trapilo, and Drew Kendall.

“I leaned on [Ezeiruaku] when it comes to everything,” Quintayvious Hutchins said. “I look at Donovan as a role model.”

Bowry discussed the influence that fellow offensive linemen Trapilo and Kendall had on his time at BC.

“It was great to have [Trapilo and Kendall] in the locker room, and, you know, in a meeting room,” Bowry said. “As leaders, and to show you what to do to get to the next level.”

O’Brien mentioned that BC alumni aren’t just valued on the Heights—they are also standing out in NFL locker rooms across the league. 

“Boston College is held in high regard,” O’Brien said, citing former Eagle Zach Allen, who visited with the team last week. “You’re talking about guys of high character, really good football players, tough guys, dependable guys … The NFL locker room is a sacred place to be. BC guys fit right in there.”

New England Patriots head coach Mike Vrabel attended Pro Day.

“It was pretty cool,” Bowry said. “It was genuine—like, [Vrabel] took the time out to seek something in me, to like, stop his day to say something to me.”

BC’s outgoing players spoke about what getting drafted would mean to them. They all shared a similar sentiment.

“It would mean everything,” Bond said. “It would just show that the work I put in for the last five years here at BC meant something.”

How do these now-former Eagles stay focused on the task at hand as the NFL Draft approaches? For Bowry, it’s all about the attitude.

“You never want to be negative,” he said. “Negativity is not going to help you get through a lot of things. So I’d rather see the positive side.”

The post BC Pro Day 2026: “It Would Mean Everything.” appeared first on The Heights.

news_bc.title: Report: Luke Murray Close to Deal with Boston College to Become Men’s Basketball Head Coach
news_bc.author: Curtis Flannery
news_bc.summary:
On Wednesday morning, a report from the New England Football Journal came out that UConn assistant Luke Murray has been offered a contract to be the next men’s basketball head coach at Boston College.

UConn is still alive in the NCAA tournament’s Sweet Sixteen, facing off against Michigan State in Washington DC on Friday March 27th at 9:45PM ET. If Murray is intent on accepting the offer from BC, he may be waiting until UConn’s season is wrapped up so that his players are not distracted in the midst of an NCAA tournament run.

Hiring Murray would be a promising start for a new era of basketball at Boston College, nabbing one of the top assistant coaches in the country from a local rival. You can read more about Murray’s background in our post from last week here. We will continue to keep track of the situation in anticipation of his acceptance. Or rejection! Go Eagles.

news_bc.title: Aram Minnetian Signs With Dallas Stars
news_bc.author: Steven Principi
news_bc.summary:
Aram Minnetian has signed an entry-level contract with the Dallas Stars, choosing to start his professional career after three seasons at Boston College.

Minnetian joined the Eagles as an impactful freshman in 2023-24 as BC fought its way to a Hockey East title and a national title game appearance. As a freshman, Minnetian scored the game-tying goal in the NCAA regional final against Quinnipiac to send BC to overtime, and then the Frozen Four!

In his three seasons at Boston College, Minnetian scored 7 goals and 27 assists and was a key player on great BC defenses. Minnetian was selected by the Stars in the fourth round of the 2023 NHL Draft. Congratulations to Aram on a successful three years at BC and good luck in Dallas!

news_bc.title: James Hagens Signs With Boston Bruins
news_bc.author: Steven Principi
news_bc.summary:
James Hagens has signed an ATO with the Boston Bruins, choosing to start his professional career after two excellent seasons at Boston College.

Hagens joined the Eagles in the 2024-2025 season as one of the highest rated freshmen in the country. He immediately lived up to the billing, putting up 37 points in 36 games in his first year at BC. He was even better as a sophomore, when he lead the team in scoring with 47 points in 34 games and making it all the way to the top 10 of Hobey Baker voting. Hagens also skated in the World Junior Championship in both of his two seasons at BC, representing the United Skates and putting up 16 points in 12 games over the two tournaments.

Hagens was selected seventh overall by the Bruins is the 2025 draft and will have a chance to join the team in the midst of a late season playoff push. Congrats to Hagens on two fantastic seasons at BC and best of luck to him as he starts his professional career.

news_bc.title: Drew Fortescue Signs With New York Rangers
news_bc.author: Steven Principi
news_bc.summary:
Drew Fortesue signed a three-year entry level contract with the New York Rangers on Saturday, ending his career at Boston College after his junior season.

Fortescue joined the Eagles as a part of a loaded freshman class in the 2023-2024 season and immediately became a key member on the team’s blueline. He was a strong defensive presence for all of his three seasons at BC and showed a bit of an offensive touch in his junior year, posting a career best 14 points in 36 games. Over the course of his three seasons, Fortescue played in 112 games, scoring eight goals and adding 25 assists in that time. He also represented the United States in the World Junior Championship in both his freshman and sophomore seasons.

Fortescue was selected in the third round by the Rangers back in 2023 and will now begin his professional career. Best of luck to Drew going forward!

news_bc.title: Coaching Candidate: Is John Becker the guy?
news_bc.author: Joseph Celia
news_bc.summary:
The coaching candidate buzz continues to headline Boston College Athletics. If the early reports hold true, the candidate can be chosen any day now. Recently, a name that’s floated around with the vacancy is no one other than John Becker. 

Becker, 57, is the current head coach of the Vermont Catamounts. Dating back to the 2006-07 season, he began as the director of basketball operations with the team before he eventually was promoted to assistant coach, By 2011, UVM promoted him as head coach. He is the winningest UVM Men’s Basketball coach of all time, currently holding a 329-132 record, along with seven American East Conference championships and six NCAA Tournament berths. 

What Becker is known for is continuing to establish a dominant program for UVM. He’s developed high IQ principles with UVM, especially when it comes to defense. His defensive culture has allowed the Catamounts to boast nine top 3 finishes in allowing the fewest points per game each season. In many games this year the Eagles’ calling card was defense, so there’s certainly some similarities as it pertains to Becker and BC’s gameplay philosophy. 

Why he’d be interested

This is an intriguing story with Becker reportedly interviewing for the position. Considering his success in the AEC, it could very well be in his best interest to continue on there. Why all of a sudden does he wish to leave a well-established program for one that’s been in turmoil for years? Personally, I think his name floating around has to do with the idea of Becker thinking he can rebuild the Eagles from the ground up. When it comes to Men’s Basketball, the ACC holds a more respected status than the AEC. Moving to the ACC with a newly built-in culture and transforming the Eagles for success is something to consider if you’re Becker. Not to mention BC could also offer more money if the price is right between both parties. 

Why it could work

UVM was a successful program to begin with, but Becker raised their ceiling even higher. Year after year he finds ways to construct hard working rosters with a knack for toughness and intensity. Player development is big with Becker, something the Eagles desperately lacked – remember when Caleb Steger, Akbar Waheed III, and Marko Radunovic were supposedly going to be key developmental pieces this year? Earl Grant for some reason refused to play most of the freshmen when games were out of reach. Becker on the other hand gives me the vibe that he’d look at the big picture and evaluate what he has to work with, even if it means playing inexperienced freshmen. 

Why it wouldn’t work

Coaching in the AEC generates lots of wins for Becker, but it does raise questions about the caliber of said wins. Winning over UMass Lowell, UNH, Bryant, and Binghamton consistently doesn’t exactly generate lots of buzz. That’s not to take away from his credibility, but facing the likes of Duke, UNC, Virginia, Louisville, and Miami is a different animal. Rebuilding Boston College (basically from scratch) is a high risk, high reward situation, but I think the risk outweighs the reward by a long shot. 

Also, the elephant in the room needs to be mentioned. Becker carries some controversy in his past, dating back to a serious incident involving one of his basketball players with a UVM women’s swimmer nearly six years ago. What kind of look would the school be setting if he does end up being the guy? 

How likely is the hire?

Out of seemingly nowhere, there is buzz with Becker reportedly a serious candidate. However, the same can be said with a few other names, so at this point it’s really difficult to pinpoint exactly who is the most likely candidate.

news_bc.title: Recap: Boston College falls to UConn in Hockey East Tournament, 4-3 (OT)
news_bc.author: Curtis Flannery
news_bc.summary:
On Friday night, the men’s Hockey East semifinals were played at TD Garden between the Boston College Eagles and UConn Huskies. After Merrimack won the early game against UMass, BC and UConn faced off for a berth in the conference championship game against the Warriors.

Play was very sloppy from the start. BC got a couple of quality opportunities on an early powerplay, but otherwise struggled to generate much offense. Mostly the Eagles were failing to even get the puck into the offensive zone. UConn had an aggressive forecheck and was clogging passing lanes, resulting in plenty of turnovers. Louka Cloutier was on his game, though, and made a number of great stops when the Huskies came at him.

On one of BC’s first period powerplays, the puck appeared to have slipped by UConn goalie Tyler Muszelik after a scramble near the net. But a referee blew his whistle while the puck was very briefly covered from his view, waving off the goal that followed a second later. And because the whistle was blown, Boston College’s bench wouldn’t have won a challenge.

The Eagles finally cashed in at the start of the second period. They began the period on a powerplay, and before too long Dean Letourneau set up a pretty shot on the left side of the net to give BC a 1-0 lead. That would be short-lived. UConn came right down the other end and trickled one by Cloutier to tie it up less than a minute later.

A few minutes after that, it was the powerplay that gave the Eagles life again. Dean Letourneau planted himself in front of the goal and Muszelik couldn’t stop the 6’7” sophomore from scoring on a redirect. BC was taking advantage of their opportunities and had a 2-1 lead to show for it. But the question remained if their 5-on-5 play could hold up. UConn answered that very quickly when Husky sophomore Ethan Whitcomb roofed a shot over Cloutier just a few minutes later, and BC burned their timeout on an unsuccessful offsides challenge. It was 2-2 halfway through the second period.

UConn controlled the game for a very long stretch after that. BC led in shots 16-9 when Letourneau put in his 2nd goal. UConn led in shots, 17-16, when finally BC earned another powerplay with 4:32 remaining in the period. They didn’t even register any shots on that powerplay, and the Eagles were lucky that the intermission arrived still with a tie score.

Cloutier continued to hold strong to start the third period. BC’s offense was nowhere to be found, but still UConn wasn’t getting enough contribution from their top forwards to finish shots. Cloutier made a few good saves, but the Huskies also blew a few key opportunities. With under ten minutes to play, Andre Gasseau drove down the middle of the ice and fired the puck right at the goalie. It was BC’s first shot on goal in 22 minutes of game time.

The dam finally burst on a UConn breakaway. Whitcomb cut to the net and slipped the puck right into Cloutier’s five-hole. He scored his second goal of the night and gave UConn a 3-2 lead with under eight minutes to play.

When all seemed lost, Ryan Conmy brought Boston College back to life. He lined-up a one-timer 20 feet in front of the net on an errant UConn pass and fired it past Muszelik for a goal, tying the game at 3-3. Despite getting pushed around all game, BC came back to one of its shotmakers and his skill kept the team’s season alive. BC got a little more offense going after that, but a missed Conmy pass to Minnetian at the top of the zone blew their best scoring chance. Regulation concluded shortly after and the game went to overtime.

Overtime was brief. Tristan Fraser pushed a shot past Cloutier’s left pad and the puck trickled into the corner of the goal. UConn won 4-3.

With the loss, Boston College’s season is over. They will finish a few spots out of the NCAA tournament after this weekend’s games. They finish with a 20-15-1 record, a Beanpot victory, and a 4th-place finish in Hockey East.

Boston College looked lost for most of the game, getting outshot 31-25 in regulation, failing to implement basic coordination on many passes and zone entries. UConn’s heavy forecheck forced too many turnovers and BC wasn’t able to punish them for playing aggressive defense. There were only small flashes of Hagens moving the puck down ice, as he mostly got pushed around by the UConn defenders and his impact was limited. The only time BC really looked comfortable was on the powerplay, which had some success, but was not enough to carry them to a victory. It’s been a disappointing stretch to end this season, and it feels like a totally different team than the one that won the Beanpot.

news_bc.title: Coach Candidate: Could Eric Olen Find Success on the East Coast?
news_bc.author: Curran Schestag
news_bc.summary:
We’re getting close to hiring time. If Matt Norlander is to be believed, Boston College is aiming to have its next head coach in place within the next few days. The names linked to the job continue to fluctuate, but one guy whose name cropped up relatively later in the process has potentially remained in the pack: Eric Olen.

Olen, 45, is in his 13th season as a head coach. He spent 12 years at the University of California, San Diego (UCSD) before replacing Richard Pitino at New Mexico prior to this season. He’s got a career record of 264-129, and he’s made one Division I NCAA Tournament appearance.

Why he’d be interested:

This is, in my opinion, the biggest question with Olen. His name was not circulated in some initial lists that cropped up immediately following Earl Grant’s firing, but once he appeared in the search he has not gone away. He’s reportedly interviewed for the job, but I have a couple questions about his interest. The first would be that he’s in his first year at New Mexico. It is generally rarer to see coaches jump after just one season at a program, though not unheard of. Is Boston College really a strong enough job to lure Olen away? The New Mexico job is one of the better and more-resourced programs in the Mountain West. He’d be fighting uphill battles in the ACC.

With that said, the obvious reason to make the jump is to break into high major ball, both to compete at the highest level and for the financial windfall as well.

Why it could work:

Flat out, Olen can coach. He was dominant early in his tenure at UCSD, winning 159 games at the DII level over seven years, while making four straight DII NCAA Tournaments (and a 30-1 squad in 2020 would’ve made it five had the tournament not been cancelled). Olen then guided the program into Division I. The transition was a bit bumpy — he posted three straight losing seasons from 2020-2023 — before breaking through with 21 wins in 2023/2024. The Tritons posted 3o wins and almost upset Michigan in the 2025 tournament before Olen left for New Mexico. He won 25 games this season, making the NIT.

He’s also shown the chops to survive in the world of revenue sharing and NIL. He arrived at New Mexico needing to virtually build a roster from scratch — not a single one of his top 5 scorers were with the program last season. Which leads me to my next point: of those top five scorers, three of them are freshmen. Jake Hall (15.9 points, 3.3 rebounds, 1.6 assists per game), Tomislav Buljan (12.8 points, 10.4 rebounds, 1.5 assists per game), and Uriah Tenette (10.7 points, 2.7 rebounds, 2.8 assists) were all key contributors to the Lobos’ success this season. If Olen took the job, it is reasonable to expect that one or some combination of them follow their coach to Chestnut Hill, providing a necessary injection of talent and familiarity for the program.

Why it wouldn’t work:

Two reasons. Firstly, Olen has never coached east of New Mexico. He’d be entering uncharted geographic territory. He would need to ingratiate himself with local high school programs, and figure out — quickly — a viable talent acquisition strategy. He’d also have to accomplish all of this with the notoriously lacking resources the BC job has.

Secondly, Olen has no experience at any level in high major ball. Prior to getting the UCSD head coaching job, Olen was an assistant there for ten years. That’s the extent of his coaching experience. That jump in level is concerning. Nevertheless, Olen’s record dictates that he will likely get a high major shot sooner rather than later.

How likely is the hire?

Since emerging as a candidate, Olen’s name has remained firmly linked to BC. He also did not exactly shut down the possibility of him taking another job this cycle when asked by reporters earlier this week. However, his name has also been linked to the Arizona State and Providence jobs — both probably more appealing to Olen. For that reason, I think Olen is in the realm of possibility but trending towards becoming more unlikely. I think he would be as great a hire as could reasonably be expected, but he’s probably the third- or fourth-mostly likely for the job at this point, in my opinion.

news_bc.title: Hockey East Playoffs Preview: Boston College vs. UConn
news_bc.author: SB Nation College
news_bc.summary:
Who: Boston College Eagles (20-14-1) vs Connecticut Huskies (19-11-5)

When: Friday, 3/20

Puck Drop: 7PM

Where:
TD Garden, Boston, MA

How to Watch: The game will be live on NESN.

UConn Preview:

  • The Eagles last met the Huskies a month ago, collecting 5/6 Hockey East points over the weekend series.
  • Senior Ryan Tattle leads the team with 31 points in 35 games, followed by junior Jake Richard, who has 27 points in 35 games.
  • In net, Tyler Muszelik has a .928 save percentage in 32 games.
  • The Huskies made it to last year’s Hockey East Championship game, eventually losing to Maine.
  • UConn’s power play is at 23.3%, while their penalty kill is at 80.0%.

What to Watch For:

  • Honestly, I don’t even know. I’m going to say essentially what I did last week, despite the Eagles’ showing against Maine. This season, we’ve seen two versions of the Eagles: an incredibly locked in one that won the team’s first Beanpot in ten seasons, and a team that has been the exact opposite. They’re going to have to decide which team shows up this Friday against the Huskies, who — along with wanting to make it back to the title game for the second year in a row — will be seeking revenge for the Eagles’ sweep. It is very, very hard to defeat any team three times in a season, let alone in a month. They’ll need to continue to remember what they’re capable of and dictate play from puck drop till the final buzzer.

news_college_hockey.title: Final Bracket Projection: Will the committee deviate from past precedent?
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Alright, here we go. The NCAA will officially unveil its bracket on ESPN-U today at 3 p.m., and with it, the final word on a season that has been anything but predictable.

Let’s dig into the final projection, and there are some interesting things that the committee might have to consider.

First, the final NPI:

  1. Michigan [AQ]

  2. North Dakota

  3. Michigan State

  4. Western Michigan

  5. Denver [AQ]

  6. Dartmouth [AQ]

  7. Providence

  8. Minnesota Duluth

  9. Penn State

  10. Quinnipiac

  11. Cornell

  12. Wisconsin

  13. Minnesota State [AQ]

  14. Connecticut

  15. Merrimack [AQ]

  16. Bentley [AQ]

On paper, a straight serpentine bracket would slot teams accordingly.

The conventional approach places No. 1 seeds closest to home. But this bracket has a wrinkle, and it starts with Denver.

As a host in Loveland, Denver is locked into that regional. That has a ripple effect, particularly on Michigan State and Western Michigan. Both are No. 1 seeds, and both are headed on the road regardless. Their placement likely hinged on Denver.

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: What I'm hearing on where things stand in Minnesota's coaching search
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Minnesota is moving with purpose in its search for a new head coach.

Just days after announcing that Bob Motzko won’t return, the Gophers have already conducted multiple virtual interviews. The process has accelerated quickly, and all indications are that it has narrowed to a short list of finalists.

This newsletter (#664) lays out everything known about the search — who Minnesota has contacted, where things stand, and what comes next.

Let’s start with the headline.

It appears Minnesota has zeroed in on Brett Larson and Grant Potulny as its two finalists. Mick Hatten reported that earlier today, and sources tell College Hockey Insider they’ve heard the same.

There have been other names connected to the search, though. Before diving deeper into the finalists — and acknowledging that these searches can always expand — it’s worth noting one candidate who is no longer in the mix.

Former Minnesota assistant and current Augustana head coach Garrett Raboin has removed himself from consideration, sources told College Hockey Insider on Friday night. Augustana athletic director Josh Morton posted a photo with Raboin that same night, captioned: “Our coach. Now and for years to come — LET’S GO!”

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: Saturday Morning NPI Update: Wisconsin, UConn, and Augustana eyeing the last at-large spots
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Minnesota State became the first program to secure a conference championship with a win over St. Thomas on Friday night. The Mavericks punched their ticket back to the NCAA Tournament as the CCHA’s automatic qualifier, continuing a run that has become familiar this time of year.

Positioning remains fluid, however. Minnesota State sits at No. 14 in the NPI entering the final night of conference championship play, with a path to climb to the No. 13 overall seed depending on how results break across the country.

With just one night left to shape the bracket, the NCAA Tournament picture — and the NPI that drives it — remains very much in motion.

This edition of the newsletter (#663) breaks down all of the scenarios.

Wisconsin is on the verge of locking up an at-large berth. The Badgers, UConn, and Augustana are all alive, but it will depend on who wins the automatic qualifiers elsewhere.

Tournament Locks

First, let’s look at the NCAA Tournament locks. I’ll include where these teams could potentially finish overall as well.

  1. Michigan (locked as the No. 1 seed)

  2. North Dakota (locked as the No. 2 seed)

  3. Michigan State (locked as the No. 3 seed)

  4. Western Michigan (locked as the No. 4 seed)

  5. Denver (No. 5-6)

  6. Minnesota Duluth (No. 5-8, but cannot be No. 6)

  7. Dartmouth (No. 6-8)

  8. Providence (No. 6-8)

  9. Penn State (No. 9-10)

  10. Quinnipiac (No. 9-10)

  11. Cornell (locked as the No. 11 seed)

As I wrote earlier this week, we can reasonably project Michigan as the No. 1 seed in Albany and North Dakota as the No. 1 seed in Sioux Falls. The placement of Michigan State and Western Michigan, however, may ultimately hinge on where Denver lands as either the No. 5 or No. 6 overall seed.

The Pioneers are slotted for Loveland as the host school. If they come in as the No. 5 seed, the bracket naturally aligns them with No. 4 Western Michigan in the opposite semifinal. If they slide to No. 6, they would instead draw No. 3 Michigan State.

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: Where things stand with the NPI and the bracket heading into conference championship weekend
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Conference championship weekend drops the puck tomorrow, and the picture is coming into sharp focus.

Heading into the weekend, today’s newsletter (#662) is fully devoted to breaking down the current state of the NPI, sorting through the bubble, and mapping out the potential placement scenarios for the NCAA Tournament bracket.

If you have ideas for a future newsletter, tips to pass along, or questions for a mailbag, I’d love to hear from you. You can reach me anytime at mike@collegehockeyinsider.com. Sponsorship inquiries are also welcome.

You can also find me on X at @MikeMcMahonCHN.

And a sincere thank you to our premium subscribers — your support makes independent, in-depth college hockey coverage possible.

Now, let’s get into today’s newsletter.

Before we get into a projected bracket, let’s take a closer look at the NPI and establish what we can definitively say at this point.

The No. 1 Seeds Are Set in Stone

Not only are the four No. 1 seeds locked in, but their exact positions — one through four — are already determined:

  1. Michigan

  2. North Dakota

  3. Michigan State

  4. Western Michigan

Three of those four programs are idle this weekend after being eliminated last week, and Michigan cannot fall below North Dakota even with a loss in the Big Ten championship game.

There’s no intrigue here. The top of the bracket is settled.

Who Is Locked Into the NCAA Tournament?

Entering the weekend, 11 of the 16 NCAA Tournament spots are secured. Wisconsin’s odds are overwhelmingly favorable — if not mathematically certain — and the Atlantic Hockey champion will claim an automatic bid. That effectively brings the number of near-certainties to 13.

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: Saturday Recap: Top seeds fall in four leagues
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Four top seeds have gone down in their conference tournaments, as Merrimack, Minnesota Duluth, Ohio State, and Clarkson pulled out wins on the road Saturday night.

Today’s issue (#661) includes all the scores from Saturday night, along with notes from every playoff game played across the country.

Current NPI » Current Standings

Merrimack 3, Providence 2 (OT): The moment arrived quickly for Merrimack, and the Warriors didn’t waste it. Trevor Hoskin delivered the decisive blow Saturday night, scoring 3:50 into overtime to lift Merrimack to a 3-2 victory over Providence in the Hockey East quarterfinals and send the Warriors to the semifinal round at TD Garden next Friday.

“[Gill] made such a great play on that goal,” Hoskin said. “He cut to the left and that made their D turn and gave me the time to take that shot. He made such a great play there.”

Merrimack will face Massachusetts in the semifinals.

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: Friday Recap: Clarkson shuts out Quinnipiac in ECAC series opener
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Friday’s playoff action was headlined by Clarkson’s 3-0 win over Quinnipiac. The Golden Knights stunned the Bobcats with a shutout victory in the opening game of their three-game ECAC series. Harvard also delivered a road win in the ECAC playoffs.

In Hockey East, Boston College snapped a four-game losing streak and brought Maine’s season to an end.

Today’s issue (#660) includes all the scores from Friday night, along with notes from every playoff game played across the country.

Current NPI » Current Standings

Clarkson 3, Quinnipiac 0: This may have been the biggest surprise of the night. Quinnipiac was shut out and outshot 35–24, as Clarkson delivered one of its most complete efforts of the season. Shane Soderwall turned aside all 24 shots he faced to record the shutout.

Bryce Sookro opened the scoring early in the second period, and from there the Golden Knights took over. Clarkson dominated the middle frame, outshooting Quinnipiac 17–2. Through 40 minutes, the Bobcats were chasing the game, trailing 1–0 and facing a 28–7 deficit in shots.

Quinnipiac pushed back in the third period, but Clarkson sealed the result with a pair of empty-net goals. The Golden Knights will look to finish the sweep Saturday.

Read more
